17. How to Raise The Value of The Price.mp4 Get a lot of pushback on this one. I always say the price first, and then I justify everything that comes with it after. If you leave price at the very end of whatever you've justified, to the person, it becomes a cliffhanger, okay? So let me be clear on this. If I say the price at the beginning of the conversation, I'm like, 'Hey, I just want to be transparent with you at the beginning of this call. Our offer is about $40,000. We typically encourage people to just have that amount liquid. If you don't, we can get you approved for plenty of funding partners that we have. But in fairness, I just want to make sure that I say that. That way, everything that follows. This point of our conversation justifies why it makes sense to move forward with us. But some people are really taken aback by that type of price point. They're not in the position to be able to work with us. So I just wanted to make sure we don't waste one another's time before we continue the conversation. If I... show you why it makes sense to make that type of investment. Do you think you could spend the next 15 minutes with me on this call walking through that process? Or is it too much of a sticker shock for you and you want to end the conversation here? Great way to start a conversation. Get bad leads off the phone, right off the rip. But in addition to that, I'm playing into a good psychology trick here. After I start with the price, the rest— of the conversation that follows— I'm consistent. So I talk about a feature. I talk about another feature. I talk about another feature. I talk about another feature. I ask them questions. I get the data and the pain points behind why they want to do it. All of a sudden, the value, the perceptual value of my offer, is much higher. And I started with the price so they can understand why it costs what it costs. Versus the opposite is also true. And unfortunately, it plays out negatively. If I start... with the value. Okay. I start with, here's what it does. Here's how it works. Let me ask you some questions. Oh, I understand your pain. Okay. We're on the same page now. Oh, here's the price. All of a sudden. There's a huge. cliff there. meaning now they're likely going to have some potential objections. There's nothing that can follow the price to justify why it costs that much. Might think, 'Oh, well, Jeremy, I mean, what the fuck are you talking about?' You know, you just said everything over here that has to do with why it's as valuable as it is. But once again, it's the sticker shock of the situation. If I'm going to buy a $300,000 car and I know right off the bat, it's $300,000. Let's say it's a new Aventador from Lamborghini. Well, I already know it's $300,000. Every other cool thing that I learned about it after I know that makes me think to myself. It makes total sense why it's worth $300,000. Versus if I learn all of the cool shit about the Lamborghini Aventador first, I learn about the exhaust. I learn about how loud it is. I learn about... That little window behind your head so you can hear the engine louder— you know, I learned about the fact it's a limited edition run. You know, I learned about the cool colors and all the paint stuff that they do. I learned about the paddle shifters and like all the the transmission and the engine, the trunks in the front, you know, all that fun stuff. But then I hear it's 300,000. Oh. Shit. Sounds expensive. Nothing can really follow that. That makes it easier to digest the fact that it costs that much. It's a great psychology trick. It works extremely well. I'd encourage you to try it out, especially if you're not doing it now, because it's very, very effective at selling higher ticket products. Lead with the price. Get the shitty people off the phone. and then add the value after you drop the price point. Try it out. Let me know how it goes for you.
